Demis Hassabis — DeepMind and the Path to AGI

Demis Hassabis, CEO of Google DeepMind

March 10, 2024
Dwarkesh Patel: Demis, you've been working on artificial general intelligence since before it was a mainstream idea. What does AGI actually mean to you? Demis Hassabis: For me AGI means a system that can learn to do any cognitive task that a human can do. Not just pattern matching or narrow optimization but genuine flexible reasoning across domains. The key word is generalization. A truly general system should be able to transfer knowledge from one domain to another the way humans do naturally. Dwarkesh Patel: When you built AlphaGo and then AlphaZero you saw systems learn from scratch and surpass human performance. How much does that experience inform your view of what's possible? Demis Hassabis: AlphaZero was genuinely transformative for my thinking. We gave it only the rules of the game and it discovered entirely new strategies that humans had never found in centuries of play. It showed that self-play with a sufficiently powerful architecture can discover knowledge that transcends human intuition. The lesson I drew was that the bottleneck is often not the underlying intelligence but the quality of the learning signal. Dwarkesh Patel: What are the main bottlenecks right now between current systems and AGI? Demis Hassabis: I think there are three main bottlenecks. First is sample efficiency. Humans can learn new concepts from a handful of examples while current systems need millions. Second is causal reasoning. Current systems are very good at correlation but struggle with counterfactual reasoning about what would have happened under different conditions. Third is long horizon planning over complex sequences of actions where each step changes the state of the world in ways that are hard to predict. Dwarkesh Patel: How concerned are you about AI safety as capabilities increase? Demis Hassabis: Extremely concerned and this is something I think about every single day. I think we're potentially building one of the most transformative and dangerous technologies in human history. The alignment problem is genuinely hard. We don't yet know how to ensure that a superintelligent system will pursue the goals we actually want rather than some proxy for those goals. And the consequences of getting this wrong could be catastrophic and irreversible. Dwarkesh Patel: What's your timeline for AGI? Demis Hassabis: I think it could come in the next decade or two but with massive uncertainty bands. I wouldn't be surprised if it's five years and I wouldn't be surprised if it's fifty years. The key insight is that the progress has been much faster than most people expected even five years ago. Dwarkesh Patel: Do you think the current large language model paradigm can scale to AGI or do we need fundamentally new architectures? Demis Hassabis: I think the current paradigm has taken us much further than most people expected but I suspect we will need new architectural ideas to reach genuine AGI. Language models are extraordinarily impressive at many tasks but they have systematic weaknesses around grounding in physical reality, causal understanding, and the ability to reliably reason about complex multi-step problems. I don't think we can just scale our way past these limitations. Dwarkesh Patel: What role does neuroscience play in your approach to AI research? Demis Hassabis: Neuroscience has been deeply influential in my work from the very beginning. My PhD was in neuroscience and I think the brain is the existence proof that general intelligence is possible. We don't need to copy the brain but we can use it as inspiration for architectural choices. The hippocampus inspired our work on experience replay in deep reinforcement learning. The prefrontal cortex inspires our thinking about working memory and executive control. Dwarkesh Patel: Thank you Demis. This has been a remarkable conversation about the future of intelligence itself. Demis Hassabis: Thank you Dwarkesh. I think the next decade is going to be the most consequential in the history of science and I hope we navigate it wisely.
